Mercedes G-Class G350 / G500 Petrol Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes G‑Class G350 / G500 Petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M113 V8 typically costs £4,500–£7,500 supply only, with the M112 V6 slightly lower at £3,500–£6,000. A used engine in good condition costs on average £2,865. Fitting adds £1,300–£1,900. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your G‑Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes G500 (M113 V8) engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the M113 is widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most robust and reliable engines. It's a simple, 3‑valve SOHC V8 that can exceed 200,000 miles with regular maintenance. The main watchpoints are valve stem seal wear, oil leaks and timing chain tensioners — all manageable with preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the G350 / G500 Petrol engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve stem seal wear (blue smoke on startup, increased oil consumption), oil leaks from camshaft position sensors and valve covers, timing chain tensioner wear (cold‑start rattle), and EGR valve clogging. The engines are fundamentally robust when properly maintained.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a G350 / G500 Petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and engine condition. A W463 G500 is a £15,000–£45,000+ vehicle — a £5,000–£6,000 recon is 11–40% of the car's value, making replacement viable in most cases. A well‑maintained, lower‑mileage example justifies replacement; a tired, high‑mileage one may not.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes G350 / G500 Petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The G500 uses the M113 (5.0‑litre V8, engine code M113.962). The G350 (or G320 in some markets) uses the M112 (3.2‑litre V6). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a G350 / G500 Petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved oil and shortened intervals (5,000–7,500 miles) are the most effective prevention. On the M113, listen for blue smoke on startup — this indicates valve stem seal wear. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, blue smoke or oil leaks, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes G‑Class G350 / G500 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across both engine families; specific costs vary by engine — see the Era Map above. A reconditioned M113 V8 engine typically costs between £4,500 and £7,500 supply only. A replacement M113 engine costs on average £2,865 in good condition, with reconditioned units reaching higher figures.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a G‑Class V8 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The G‑Class engine bay is spacious but the weight of the V8 requires specialist equipment.

cta.label: → Compare G350 / G500 Petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Valve Stem Seal Wear (M113 & M112 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Blue smoke on startup (particularly after idling), excessive oil consumption (1L per 1,000 miles+), fouled spark plugs, occasional misfire.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before catalytic converter damage)
problems.recommendation: Valve stem seal wear is the most common issue on the M113 V8. The seals harden over time, allowing oil to leak into the combustion chamber on startup. If you see blue smoke on startup or notice increasing oil consumption, have the valve stem seals inspected. Replacing them is labour‑intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement. Some owners report that using high‑mileage oil can temporarily reduce consumption, but the only permanent fix is seal replacement.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Camshaft Sensors & Valve Covers (M113 & M112)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, oil residue on the engine, oil leaking into the wiring harness.
problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are a common issue on the M113 as it ages. Camshaft position sensors can leak oil into the wiring harness, potentially causing electrical gremlins. Valve cover gaskets also degrade over time. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the engine inspected. Replacing gaskets and seals promptly prevents more serious damage and expensive wiring harness repairs.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (M113 & M112)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start (lasting 1–3 seconds), metallic ticking, engine warning light with timing‑related fault codes (P0016, P0017, P0018).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M113's timing chain tensioners are a known wear point — they rely on oil pressure to maintain tension. If the tensioner fails, the chain can develop slack, resulting in a characteristic rattle on cold start. If left unaddressed, the chain can jump a tooth, risking bent valves and costly repairs. Regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved oil are essential. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the timing chain system inspected immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: G350 / G500 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.problem: Oil leaks — cam sensors & valve covers

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — before chain jumps
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This G350 / G500 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * M113 (G500): no blue smoke on startup — valve stem seals in good condition
* No cold‑start rattle — timing chain tensioners in good condition
* No oil leaks — camshaft sensors and valve covers dry
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes
avoidIf: * Blue smoke on startup — valve stem seals may be failing — walk away
* Cold‑start rattle with no tensioner history — timing chain issues likely
* Oil leaks with no diagnosis — potentially expensive
* Poor service history — M113 engines are robust but sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M113 V8 is one of Mercedes' most robust and reliable engines — a 5.0‑litre, 3‑valve SOHC V8 that delivers effortless torque and legendary durability. The M112 V6 is equally robust but less common. Both are chain‑driven, relatively simple and capable of high mileage. The main watchpoints — valve stem seals, oil leaks and timing chain tensioners — are well‑understood and manageable. Buy on full service history, check for blue smoke on startup, and never ignore a cold‑start rattle. A well‑maintained G500 is a future classic; a neglected one is a £5,000+ bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare G350 / G500 Petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes G‑Class G350 / G500 Petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling; timing chain tensioner indicator
* Blue Smoke — Check on startup; valve stem seal wear indicator
* Oil Leaks — Check under the car and around the engine; camshaft sensors and valve covers
* Service History — including which engine (M112 or M113) and confirmed oil‑change intervals
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? Valve stem seal indicator
* Engine Vibration — Check for rough idle or misfire; timing or ignition issues possible
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing or sensor issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
